persona_description: 22-year-old female student, mentally healthy, opened the app out of curiosity after a lecture on mental health.
46-year-old employed male who follows wellness trends and wants to learn more about mental health topics for self-development.
58-year-old retired woman, generally content, previously did short-term therapy years ago, now exploring the app to stay informed.
15-year-old student who prefers not to disclose gender; mildly stressed about school exams but considers it normal, had brief counseling at school before.
29-year-old unemployed woman with persistent low mood and hopelessness for over 6 months; previously diagnosed with depression.
42-year-old employed man experiencing gradual loss of enjoyment in work and hobbies, trouble concentrating, and disrupted sleep for the past 2 months.
24-year-old female student with severe generalized anxiety and OCD rituals that significantly interfere with academic performance; currently in therapy.
36-year-old employed man dealing with persistent intrusive thoughts and checking compulsions that take up several hours per day.
17-year-old male student struggling with sustained attention, frequently losing track in class and forgetting tasks; teachers have flagged academic decline.
31-year-old employed woman who has struggled with focus and task initiation throughout her career; previously evaluated but not formally diagnosed.
26-year-old employed male who has always found social interactions exhausting and confusing; reports difficulty forming relationships and recent low mood.
34-year-old employed woman with trauma history; reports severe mood swings, relationship breakdowns, persistent nightmares, and passive thoughts of self-harm.
20-year-old female student experiencing severe depression with hopelessness and recent self-harm ideation; has not previously sought professional help.
33-year-old employed man who reports being 'fine' but acknowledges moderate emotional interference with daily tasks in recent weeks; borderline relaxed.
48-year-old unemployed person (prefers not to disclose gender) who insists they feel 'fine' but PHQ-2 scores indicate frequent depressive symptoms; borderline concerned.
